title: Ogueri v Federal Republic of Nigeria (ECW/CCJ/APP/13/21; ECW/CCJ/JUD/43/25) [2025] ECOWASCJ 40 (10 November 2025)

THE COMMUNITY COURT OF JUSTICE OF THE 
ECONOMIC CO:MMUNITY OF WEST AFRICAN STATES (ECOWAS) 
HOLDEN AT ABUJA, NIGERIA 
Application No. ECW/CCJ/APP/13/21; Judgment No. ECW/CCJ/JUD/43/25

VINCENT OGUERI 
FEDERAL REPUBLIC OF NIGERIA 
AND

2 


I. 
JUDGMENT 
1. This is a judgment of the Court read virtually in open court pursuant to Article 
8(1) of the Practice Directions on Electronic Case Management and Virtual Court 
Sessions, 2020. 
II. 
DESCRIPTION OF THE PARTIES 
2. Applicant, Mr Vincent Ogueri, is a Nigerian citizen on death row in a maximum 
security prison at Enugu, in the Federal Republic of Nigeria. 
3. Respondent, the Federal Republic ofNigeria, is an ECOWAS member state. 
III. 
INTRODUCTION 
e,• 
~<.; 
Subject Matter ol,tlie Proceedings 
.._o 
~ 
4. The Applicant alleges that he is ~~-year-old man (at the date of the application), 
~ 
who has been on death row ~it Nigerian prison for about 30 years, where he is 
<..? 
being held under extremely inhuman or degrading conditions that have affected 
his health. Applicant has therefore approached the Court for reliefs including an 
order for his immediate release from detention and payment of 55 million naira 
as compensation to him. 
IV. 
PROCEDURE BEFORE THE COURT 
5. The Applicant and two NGOs initiated this proceeding by an Application dated 
26 March 2021 and filed at the Registry of the Court on 3 0 March 2021. The 
Application was served on the Respondent the same day. 
3 


6. On 8 July 2021, Respondent filed a Notice of Preliminary Objection and 
Respondent's Statement of Defence both dated 31 May 2021. 
The two 
documents were served on Applicants the same day, 8 July 2021. 
7. On 3 May 2024, Applicants filed a response to Respondent's preliminary 
objection together with a Reply to the Statement of Defence. Both processes were 
served electronically on Respondent the same day. 
8. At a virtual session of the Court on 6 May 2024, the Court noted that Applicants 
had just filed their responses to the Respondent's Preliminary Objection and 
Defence. It adjourned the case to enable Respondent file its rejoinder. 
9. On 21 June 2024, Applicants filed an application for extension of time to 
regularize their replies to Respondent's pr~
inary objection and Statement of 
C 
Defence. The application was electroni~ 
served on the Respondent on 24 June 
\❖' 
2024. 
~~ 
t,O.;s 
-~~ 
,;:,,;:-" 
IO.At a hearing of the case one,,~ June 2024, at which all parties were represented 
by Counsel, the Court heard Applicants' application for extension of time which 
was not opposed by Respondent. The Court granted the application and deemed 
Applicants' processes as regularly filed. The Court then heard the parties on the 
Respondent's preliminary objection and adjourned for deliberation and ruling. 
11.On 7 November 2024, the Court delivered Ruling No. ECW/CCJ/RUL/05/24 
dismissing the Respondent's preliminary objections and affirming its jurisdiction 
over the Application. The Court also determined that the Application was 
admissible, but struck off the NGOs Incorporated Trustees of Centre for Peace 
and Conflict Management in Africa and Rethink Africa Foundation for lacking 
standing to sue. 
4 


12.At a session of the Court on 11 March 2025 during which the Applicant's counsel 
was absent but the Respondent was represented, the Court heard the Respondent's 
arguments on the merits of the case and adjourned for deliberation and judgment. 
V. 
APPLICANT'S CASE 
a. Summary of Facts 
13 .According to the Applicant, he was convicted in absentia by the Imo State High 
Court on a charge of murder and sentenced to death by hanging in 1991. 
14. He states that he was not afforded the opportunity to defend himself with a 
counsel of his choice at the trial. Nor was adequate time or facility accorded him 
~ 
for his defence leading to his hasty convic~ 
for murder. He is currently being 
~ 
held at Enugu Maximum Security Prisgp-~ d has been on death row for about 30 
~\~ 
0 
years. 
,;;?:-
ivo 
-~ 
~ 
~~ 
~ 
15.Applicant says that he is ma:ttied and had five children, but one of them has died 
due to his absence from his family. 
16.According to the Applicant, he was scheduled for execution sometime in 2003 
along with three other death row prisoners. However, he was spared due to the 
intervention of one Moses Adekunle. 
17.Since then, the Applicant who was 72 years old (at the time of the Initiating 
Application) says that his health has deteriorated. He has an eye condition which 
is worsening into blindness. Although he has been recommended for complete 
pardon by the Nigerian Correctional Services, he is still being held on death row 
under depressing and traumatizing conditions. 
5 


b. Pleas in Law 
18. By way of pleas in law, Applicant makes the following submissions: 
(i) 
That he has been subjected to extreme mental torture and inhuman or 
degrading treatment. 
(ii) 
That the continued denial of access to medical attention while on death 
row is inhuman and violates his right to health. 
(iii) 
That given his age, his long stay on death row and his present medical 
condition, he is entitled to be released from prison forthwith. 
c. Reliefs Sought 
19.ln view of the foregoing, Applicant requests the Court for the following reliefs: 
(i) 
(ii) 
A declaration that the Applicant has suffered extreme mental torture, 
~ 
inhuman and degrading conditit~aving been kept on death row for 
~ 
more than 30 years and withgat' access to adequate medical treatment. 
~\.::; 
,.,_o 
~' 
<.,O 
A declaration that tbj~ pplicant by virtue of his age and long stay on 
f:'<f:' 
death row and p~ sently suffering from chronic medical condition is 
entitled to be released forthwith from detention. 
(iii) 
An order for immediate release of the Applicant from detention 
forthwith. 
(iv) 
A declaration that the consistent and continued denial of the right to 
adequate medical attention and rights to the Applicant in prison under 
degrading and harsh conditions is in violation of The Federal Republic 
of Nigeria constitution and Article 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7 and 26 of the African 
Charter on Human and People's Rights. 
6 


(v) 
An order directing the [Respondent] to pay a monetary compensation 
of 55,000,000 (Fifty-Five Million naira) to the Applicant for damages 
suffered as a result oflong years he has been kept in prison under cruel, 
degrading, and inhuman condition. 
(vi) 
An order for reparations, including physical, psychological, social and 
economic rehabilitation in respect of the violation of the Applicant's 
human rights and the cost of this suit which is accessed at 15,000,000 
naira (Fifteen million naira). 
(vii) 
An order directing the [Respondent] to faithfully and fully implement 
its obligations under its own co~tion and the African Charter on 
0 
Human and Peoples' Right a,~e.ten as resolutions on Moratorium on 
~ 
executions adopted recept!Y by both the African Commission on 
o"' 
Human and People'~~ghts and the Third Committee of the UN 
,§' 
~ 
GeneralAssembbf 
VI. 
RESPONDENT'S CASE 
a. Statement of Facts 
20. Respondent denies that it has violated the Applicant, Mr Vincent Ogueri's 
fundamental human rights. 
21.Respondent states that although Applicant alleges that he was tried in absentia for 
murder and sentenced to death, he has failed to attach any evidence of an appeal 
against the conviction. Nor did the Applicant ever complain or petition any 
authority about being tried in absentia during or after the trial. 
7 


22.Also, despite the fact that the time limited for appeal of the conviction has 
expired, there are provisions under Nigerian law for extension of time to 
accommodate a late appeal. Consequently, the Applicant has not been denied the 
right to appeal his 1991 conviction. Instead, he has voluntarily chosen not to 
exercise it. 
23 .Respondent further states that the Applicant was not denied a right to counsel, as 
he was represented in court by lawyer of his own choice during the trial. 
24. The Respondent also denies that the Applicant has been recommended for pardon 
by the Nigerian Correctional Services and states that because the Applicant is a 
convicted murderer, releasing him from prison will be a threat to society at large. 
~ 
0~ 
C 
25. Respondent denies that the Applicant i~~ a poor health condition. It states that 
~ 
:->" 
the prison where the Applicant is betn~ held has a medical facility where inmates 
o" 
with health conditions are treat~~In cases where a particular medical condition 
,:> 
cannot be treated at the faciijtf, the Medical Officer at the prison's medical issues 
a referral to the prison authorities for the inmate to be sent to a medical centre 
that has the capacity to treat the inmate. 
26. Respondent, therefore, says that the failure of the Applicant to attach any medical 
report from the prison medical facility to support his allegations show that 
whatever health condition he has is not beyond the capacity of the medical centre 
at the Enugu Maximum Security Prison. 
27 .Respondent further states that the Applicant is provided with palatable food and 
water in prison and that any claim to the contrary is false. Regarding the death 
warrant, the Respondent states that the decision to sign the warrant for the 
execution of a death row inmate is within the exclusive purview of State 
8 ~ ~~ 
~ 


Governors, as such, it is not a matter within the jurisdiction of the federal 
government of Nigeria. 
28.Finally, the Respondent states that the claims made in the Application are cooked 
up stories that are not supported by evidence. Accordingly, the Court should 
dismiss the case, given that, in any event, the ECOWAS Court has no jurisdiction 
to compel a Member States of ECOWAS including the Respondent to grant 
pardons to convicted persons. 
b. Pleas in Law 
29. The Respondent makes the following pleas in law on the merits of the case: 
1. 
That the Respondent has not violated any of the fundamental 
human rights of the Applic~Punder the African Charter. 
(,0 
ii. 
That the Applicant is n,f entitled to the reliefs sought in the 
~ 
'.\)' 
Application. 
~ ~ ') 
~ 
(,0 
.~ 
~ 
~.;:; 
0~ 
c.c Reliefs Sought 
30.Respondent therefore requests the Court to dismiss the suit for non-disclosure of 
a reasonable cause of action against the Respondent. 
VIl. PROCEEDINGS BEFORE THE COURT 
31. The Respondent raised preliminary objections to the jurisdiction of the Court and 
admissibility of the application which were heard on 28 June 2024. In Ruling No. 
ECW /CCJ/RUL/05/24, delivered by the Court on 7 November 2024, the Court 
dismissed the Respondent's preliminary objections to jurisdiction and 
admissibility of the Application. It however determined that NGOs Incorporated 
Trustees of Centre for Peace and Conflict Management in Africa and Rethink 
9 
©> ~ ~ 
~ 


Africa Foundation, who filed the suit alongside Mr Vincent Oguerri, had no 
standing to sue and therefore struck them off the Application. 
vm. JURISDICTION OF THE COURT 
32. The Court affirms its jurisdiction over the Application consistent with Ruling No. 
ECW/CCJ/RUL/05/24 delivered on 7 November 2024. 
IX. 
ADMISSIBILITY OF THE CASE 
33.In line with Ruling No. ECW/CCJ/RUL/05/24 delivered on 7 November 2024, 
the Court affirms that the Application is admissible. 
;, 
0-:i-
«,G 
e• 
X. 
MERITS 
~$'c; 
!<,. ~ 
34.Having regard to the pleadings int~ ~ pplication including the reliefs sought, the 
c.,0 
Applicant makes the follow~
-.c\.substantive claims of human rights violations 
0,$' 
which the Court must address: 
(a) That the Applicant was tried, convicted and sentenced in absentia 
and without guarantees of fair trial under the African Charter. 
(b) That the Applicant has been subjected to torture and cruel, 
inhuman or degrading treatment during his long imprisonment on 
death row. 
( c) That the Applicant has been consistently denied adequate medical 
attention in violation of his rights under the African Charter. 
3 5. The Court will now consider each of these alleged human rights violations in turn. 
10 


(a)Alleged trial, conviction and sentence of the Applicant in 
absentia and without guarantees of fair trial under the 
African Charter 
(i) Submissions of the Applicant 
36.On this issue, the Applicant submits that he was tried by the Imo State High Court 
in absentia and without any legal representation on the charge of murder in 1991. 
He was then sentenced to death by hanging in what was a hasty trial without the 
opportunity and facilities for him to defend himself. Further, he was denied the 
time and facility to appeal even when subsequent investigations disclosed 
exculpatory evidence. 
~~ 
(ii) Submissions of the J/;espondent 
. ,.,e• 
37. The Respondent denies that the Applie'fult was tried in absentia on the charge of 
0 
murder without legal representatio,f ·or other guarantees of fair trial. Further, that 
-;::.4-
~ 
at no point was the Applic~l denied the right to appeal his conviction and 
<wo 
sentence. Respondent, therefore, submits that the Applicant's claims on this issue 
are unsupported by any evidence and are without merit. 
(iii) Analysis of the Court 
38. The Court notes that the alleged trial of the Applicant in absentia occurred in 
1991, several years before this Court obtained its human rights jurisdiction in 
2005. However, as of the date of the alleged trial, the African Charter on Human 
and Peoples' Rights which the Respondent ratified in 1983 and which the 
Applicant relies on in this case, was binding on the Respondent, having entered 
into force on 21 October 1986. The Applicant remains in prison under the death 
sentence pronounced on him during the alleged trial in absentia. It was because 
of this continuing imprisonment on death row resulting, allegedly, from an 


unlawful trial that the Court ruled that it had jurisdiction. See Centre for Peace 
and Conflict Management & Others v Federal Republic of Nigeria 
[ECW/CCJ/RUL/05/24], para 32. 
39.Having examined the evidence on the merits, the Court concludes that the 
Applicant has not presented any credible evidence to substantiate the claims that 
he was tried in absentia, denied the right to counsel, the right to appeal, or other 
guarantees of a fair trial. The Court notes that the only evidence submitted in 
support of these claims is a witness statement made by one Jude Arthur Angel, a 
staff member of Rethink Africa Foundation, one of the two NGOs struck off the 
case for lack of standing, rather than by the Applicant himself. This statement, 
based on second-hand information about the Applicant's trial many years after 
the fact, merely repeats the allegations set o~'tn the Application. 
~c..,O 
.,c.,"'' 
40.No judgment of the domestic court orti cord of proceedings has been submitted 
~o 
to support the claim that the Ap~i.ekt was absent during the trial or lacked legal 
~' 
representation. Nor has the#ourt been presented with any evidence that an 
c.,0 
attempt was made to obtain such documents from the relevant judicial authorities 
of the Respondent State, or that any such request was refused. 
41.For these reasons, and in line with the general principle that a party who makes a 
claim must prove it, the Court holds that the Applicant has not proved that he was 
tried in absentia or denied legal representation and other fair trial rights when he 
was tried, convicted, and sentenced to death for murder by the Imo State High 
Court in 1991. That claim is accordingly dismissed. 
12 


(b)Alleged subjection of the Applicant to torture and 
cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment during his long 
imprisonment on death row 
(i) Submissions of the Applicant 
42. The Applicant states that he has spent several years in prison on death row under 
tense and psychological trauma. Firstly, he contends, relying on various 
international jurisprudence, that the mandatory imposition of the death penalty 
on him in accordance with the extant laws of the Respondent violates his right to 
life and freedom from torture, inhuman or degrading punishment. He further 
argues that his prolonged detention on death row constitutes torture and cruel, 
inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment in violation of his rights under the 
African Charter and the ICCPR. 
tf' 
0~ 
«,u 
. e,'l-' 
~ 
(ii) Submissions ff the Respondent 
0-.:; 
43.The Respondent submits that th~){pplicant has never been tortured or subjected 
,§' 
to any form of cruel, inhu~~or degrading treatment or punishment. It therefore 
denies all allegations of torture or ill-treatment and demands strict proof from the 
Applicant. 
(iii) Analysis of the Court 
44.Article 5 of the African Charter and Article 7 of the ICCPR, respectively prohibit 
torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment. The Applicant 
contends that the sentence of death by hanging imposed on him and for which he 
has been held on death row for over 30 years under psychological and mental 
anguish, violates the prohibition of torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading 
treatment or punishment. 
13 


45. The Court notes that while there is no definitive prohibition of the death penalty 
under international law, it is generally accepted that the manner or methods of its 
implementation may violate international human rights norms including the right 
to life and the prohibition of torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or 
punishment. 
46.Accordingly, it has, for instance, been held that a mandatory imposition of the 
death penalty violates the right to life. In Dexter Johnson v Ghana (Comm No. 
2177/2012), the Human Rights Committee held that the mandatory imposition of 
the death penalty for various offences based on "the category of crime for which 
the offender is found guilty, with no margin for the judge to evaluate the 
circumstances of the particular offence" violates the right to life under Article 
6(1) of the ICCPR. The Committee noted~ paragraph 7.3 of its decision as 
~ "{-
follows: 
«,Go 
• <.,fl,' 
~ 
~.,, 
In this context, the Commit1£\ refers to its jurisprudence to the effect that the 
automatic and mandatiisf '' imposition of the death penalty constitutes an 
arbitrary deprivation ;_..~ life, in violation of article 6, paragraph 1, of the 
Covenant, in circ~ces where the death penalty is imposed without regard 
to the defendant's-
0personal circumstances or the circumstances of the particular 
offence. The existence of a de facto moratorium on the death penalty is not 
sufficient to make a mandatory death sentence consistent with the Covenant. 
47.Similarly, the African Court has held that a mandatory imposition of the death 
penalty or its execution by a method that is degrading or involve extreme 
suffering constitutes violations of the right to life and of the prohibition of torture 
or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment. Accordingly, in Rajabu 
and Others v Tanzania (merits and reparations) (2019) 3 AfCLR 539, the African 
Court held that a mandatory sentence of death by hanging violates Article 4 of 
the African Charter on the right to life and Article 5 of the Charter which prohibits 
torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment. The Court 
stated at paragraphs 118 and 119 of the judgment: 
14 


[118] The Court notes that, in the present case, the Applicants challenge the 
implementation by hanging of the death penalty as imposed in their case. The 
Court observes that many methods used to implement the death penalty have 
the potential of amounting to torture, as well as cruel, inhuman and degrading 
treatment given the suffering inherent thereto. In line with the very rationale for 
prohibiting methods of execution that amount to torture or cruel, inhuman and 
degrading treatment, the prescription should therefore be that, in cases where 
the death penalty is permissible, methods of execution must exclude suffering 
or involve the least suffering possible. 
[ 119] The Court observes that hanging a person is one of such methods and it is 
therefore inherently degrading. Furthermore, having found that the mandatory 
imposition of the death sentence violates the right to life due to its arbitrary 
nature, this Court finds that. as the method of implementation of that sentence. 
hanging inevitably encroaches upon dignity in respect of the prohibition of 
torture and cruel. inhuman and degrading treatment. 
48.On the issue of prolonged detention on death row, the Court observes that such 
detention accompanied by harsh conditions or other aggravating factors may 
constitute a violation of the prohibition of t~e or cruel, inhuman or degrading 
C 
treatment or punishment. In this regard,~European Court ofHuman Rights has 
:->' 
held that the factors that may brin~ <the death penalty within the prohibition of 
<..,O 
torture and cruel, inhuman or d~ading treatment or punishment under Article 3 
~v 
of the European Conventiotf include "[t]he manner in which it is imposed or 
executed, the personal circumstances of the condemned person and a 
disproportionality to the gravity of the crime committed, as well as the conditions 
of detention awaiting execution." (Soering v United Kingdom, ECtHR, App No. 
14038/88, para 104). 
49. Other human rights bodies have followed this approach. In Albert Wilson v. The 
Philippines (HRC, Comm No. 868/1999), the Human Rights Committee held that 
the complainant's "mental suffering and anguish as a consequence of being 
sentenced to death" was "exacerbated by his treatment in, as well as the 
conditions of, his detention, and resulted in documented long-term psychological 
damage to him" (para. 7.4). Therefore, the Committee concluded, that in view of 
15 


those "aggravating factors constituting further compelling circumstances beyond 
the mere length of time spent by the [complainant] in imprisonment under a 
sentence of death," the Respondent State had violated his right to freedom from 
torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment under Article 7 
of the ICCPR (ibid). 
50.It follows, that generally, international human rights jurisprudence has not 
accepted that the mental or psychological anguish resulting from the mere length 
of time spent in prison under a death sentence, known as the "death row 
phenomenon", constitutes torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or 
punishment. (See Johnson v Jamaica [HRC, Comm No. 588/1994]; Francis v 
Jamaica, [HRC, Comm No. 606/1994]). The Court notes, however, that this 
position presumes that the time spent on Jeath row is pursuant to a lawful 
0 
sentence of death, one that is substantiv~~<v~alid under international law and is to 
~ 
be carried out in a manner compliaJ!.!~ ith international human rights norms. 
J-
c.,0 
-~ 
~ 
... 
51.Given the above, a different~proach must apply if the psychological or mental 
c.,0 
anguish resulting from prolonged detention on death row stems from a death 
sentence imposed in violation of the State's human rights obligations. A similar 
approach is also required where the sentence is to be carried out by a method 
contrary to international human rights norms, such as the prohibition of torture or 
cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment or punishment. In such cases, it cannot be 
said that the condemned prisoner is merely complaining about suffering 
incidental to, or necessarily arising from, a lawful sentence. The prolonged 
mental pain or anguish suffered by a condemned prisoner awaiting execution of 
an unlawful sentence of death must, in and of itself, be sufficient to constitute 
torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment. 
16 


52.Accordingly, the Court holds that where a death sentence is unlawful, such as 
when it is mandatorily imposed in violation of the prisoner's right to life (see 
Dexter Johnson v. Ghana), or where the method of execution (e.g., hanging) is 
cruel, inhuman, or degrading due to the extreme suffering it causes (see Rajabu 
and Others v. Tanzania), the psychological or mental anguish suffered by the 
condemned prisoner on death row, with the unlawful sentence of death hanging 
over his head, constitutes torture or cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment or 
punishment, without the need for any aggravating factors. 
53 .Regarding the facts of the present case, the Court notes that, independent of the 
lawfulness of the trial to which the Applicant was subjected, it is not in dispute 
that the Applicant was handed a mandatory sentence of death by hanging upon 
~ 
his conviction, in accordance with the Res~dent's law. Based on the relevant 
~ 
principles of international human right~~w, as discussed above and reflected in 
K \.;;, 
various international jurispruden~including Soering v. United Kingdom 
e,O 
(ECtHR, App No. 14038/88)i 1'exter Johnson v. Ghana (HRC, Comm No. 
~ 
2177/2012); and Rajabu ani:f Others v. Tanzania (Merits and Reparations) (2019) 
3 AfCLR 539-the Court considers that the mandatory sentence of death imposed 
on the Applicant, as well as its method of execution by hanging, violates the 
Respondent's obligations under the African Charter to respect the right to life and 
to refrain from torture or cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment. 
54. The Applicant has spent over 30 years in prison under inevitable psychological 
and mental anguish arising from the sentence of death by hanging imposed on 
him. Given that both the mandatory death sentence and the method of its 
execution are contrary to the Respondent's human rights obligations, the Court 
concludes that the Applicant has been subjected to the severe mental pain or 
suffering of an unlawful sentence of death by hanging for over 3 0 years. In the 
Court's view, this constitutes torture and cruel, inhuman, or degrading treatment 
17 e, ~ ~-
~ 


or punishment, contrary to the Respondent's obligations under the African 
Charter. Accordingly, the Court holds that the Respondent has violated Article 5 
of the African Charter. 
(c) Alleged denial of adequate medical attention to the 
Applicant in violation of his rights under the Mrican 
Charter 
(i) Submissions of the Applicant 
55.0n this issue, the Applicant, who was 72 years old at the date of the Application, 
claims that his health condition is very critical due to his age and ill health, which 
may cost him his life. The Applicant states tha!,he has chronic medical conditions 
~~ 
that come with complications including JiBtential blindness. Despite these, the 
~(.; 
Applicant says that he has not been aff &rded access to adequate medical treatment 
~o 
by the prison authorities of the ~~ondent. 
~ 
~" 
<.,O~ 
(ii) Submissions of the Respondent 
56.The Respondent submits that the prison where the Applicant is being held has a 
medical facility where inmates with health conditions are treated. That the 
Medical Officer at the prison may refer an inmate for treatment outside of the 
prison if their condition cannot be handled at the prison's medical facility. 
According to the Respondent, the Applicant has failed to prove that he has not 
received medical attention in the prison or that his health condition is beyond the 
capacity of the medical centre at the Enugu Maximum Security Prison. 
Respondent therefore denies that the Applicant has not been afforded adequate 
medical attention. 
18 


(iii) Analysis of the Court 
57.The Court begins by recalling Article 16 of the African Charter which provides: 
1. Every individual shall have the right to enjoy the best attainable state of 
physical and mental health. 
2. State parties to the present Charter shall take the necessary measures to 
protect the health of their people and to ensure that they receive medical 
attention when they are sick. 
58. The Court notes that this provision guarantees the right to health to every 
individual regardless of their status or condition including detention. It follows 
that all persons including prisoners are equally entitled to enjoy the right to the 
best attainable state of health. In this regard, the United Nations Minimum 
Standard Rules for the Treatment of Prisoners, which the African Commission in 
its Resolution ACHPR/Res.466(LXVII) 2020 
called on State parties to the 
African Charter to implement, provides in r1 ¢vant part as follows: 
<.t.,c.,O 
Rule 24(1): The pro\j_sfon of health care for prisoners is a State 
responsibility. Prism~ 
should enjoy the same standards of health care 
that are available itfthe community and should have access to necessary 
health-care se~~ es free of charge without discrimination on the 
grounds of !_ll81i- legal status. 
c.,0~ 
Rule 27(1): All prisons shall ensure prompt access to medical attention 
in urgent cases. Prisoners who require specialized treatment or surgery 
shall be transferred to specialized institutions or to civil hospitals. Where 
a prison service has its own hospital facilities, they shall be adequately 
staffed and equipped to provide prisoners referred to them with 
appropriate treatment and care. 
59. The Court now turns to the facts and evidence to evaluate whether the Applicant 
has been denied adequate medical attention in violation of his right to health. The 
Court takes note of the Applicant's "Exhibit A" which seems to be a Medical 
Report on a letterhead of the University ofNigeria Teaching Hospital. However, 
the document is not legible, and it is not possible to determine the patient in 
respect of whom the report was issued or the content of the said report. 
Accordingly, the Court is not able to rely on this report in its analysis. 
19 


60. Turning to the Applicant's Exhibit D, the Court notes that this is a document titled 
"Prison Form 18, Certified True Extract From the Prison Records, Application 
For Remission of Sentence". The document which is signed by the 
Superintendent of Prisons states the Applicant's condition of health as follows: 
"He is a glaucoma patient. Frail looking with deteriorated health condition". The 
"recommendation by the Prison Authority", as can be read from the F onn is that 
he should be granted a total pardon to "enable his family members take care of 
his deteriorated health condition" outside of custody. 
61.The Court observes that the Respondent's claim that the Applicant is receiving 
adequate medical attention in prison stands in direct contrast to this evidence from 
its own Prison Authorities showing that the Applicant's health condition can be 
better managed outside of prison with the su~ort of his family. This was a key 
~ 
reason why the Respondent's own P~on Authorities recommended the 
. (,e• 
Applicant for pardon. Apart from i~->'6~e assertions, the Respondent has not 
.,_o 
~ 
produced any evidence in the f~~'bfmedical records of the Applicant (while he 
~ 
has been in its custody and ~er its medical care) to controvert the Applicant's 
~o 
well supported claim that he is not receiving adequate medical attention in the 
Enugu Maximum Security Prison. 
62.For these reasons, the Court holds that the Respondent has violated the 
Applicant's right to health by its failure to provide adequate medical care to the 
Applicant while in detention at the Enugu Maximum Security Prison. 
XI. 
REPARATIONS 
63.Having determined that the Respondent has violated the Applicant's right to 
freedom from torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment, 
and his right to health, the Court now considers reparations. 
20 


64.Reparations may take the form of restitution, compensation, satisfaction, or a 
combination of these. In this case, in addition to the declarations sought, the 
Applicant has requested monetary compensation totalling 55 million naira for the 
violation of his rights. He also requested, by way of restitution, an order for his 
release. 
65. The Court notes that in the award of general damages, it is not possible to place 
a monetary value on the suffering, distress, embarrassment, and other 
psychological harms a victim of a human rights violation would have suffered. 
Thus, while the Court may take account of factors such as the gravity or 
egregiousness of the violation and the conduct of the State in the aftermath of the 
violation, ultimately, it is up to the Court to exercise its discretion in equity to 
determine what amounts to a fair compensa11~ to be paid. 
«-~o 
• <,/l,' 
66. In this case, given the prolonged per,\b'f"fl of detention on death row for over 30 
0 
years in violation of the Applic~{';-s rights and his deteriorated health, partly 
-~ 
resulting from inadequate meMi al attention in prison, the Court considers it fair 
cj>~ 
and just to make the following award of compensation in favour of the Applicant: 
(i) the sum of 10 million naira for the violation of the Applicant's right to freedom 
from torture and cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment, and (ii) 
the sum of l O million naira for the violation of the Applicant's right to health. 
67. The Court grants other reliefs sought by the Applicant only to the extent indicated 
in the operative clause of this judgment. 
XII. COSTS 
68.Pursuant to Article 66(4) of the Rules of the Court, the Court decides that each 
party shall bear their own costs. 
21 


XIII. OPERATIVE CLAUSE

1.

Ruling No. ECW/CCJ/RUL/05/24 delivered on 7 November 2024. 
On admissibility 
11. 
Affirms that the Application is admissible consistent with its Ruling No. 
ECW/CCJ/RUL/05/24 delivered on 7 November 2024. 
On the merits 
iii.

legal representation and other fair trial rights when he was tried, convicted, 
and sentenced to death for murder by !I® Imo State High Court in 1991. 
~Q

~

mandatory sentence of deat9_~ y hanging constitutes torture and cruel,

,::,

v.

African Charter.

grounds.

conditions.
